coredata.dc:title: Colorimetric/fluorescence dual-mode sensor based on acid phosphatase/peroxidase-mimicking cascade catalysis for ultrasensitive detection of tartaric acid
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Xin
Liu, Xin
Lin, Ziting
Sun, Guangze
Bian, Zihan
Liu, Huan
Liu, Aihua
coredata.dc:description: Nanozymes hold great promise in catalysis-based sensing, however, challenges remain due to their less specificity and unclear catalytic mechanisms, which limited their potential applications. Herein we report an ultrasensitive colorimetric/fluorescence dual-mode tartaric acid (TA) sensor based on hydroxy-iron oxide (FeOOH) nanorods (NRs)@carbon dots (CDs) nanocomposite incorporating with acid phosphatase (ACP). The peroxidase (POD)-like activity of FeOOH can catalyze H2O2 to generate OH, which can further oxidize 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to produce blue oxTMB, while the fluorescence of CDs can be quenched by the assembled FeOOH NRs through internal filter effect. The FeOOH@CDs can effectively transfer electron enabling high catalytic activity, which is supported by Cytochrome c as an electron transporter. ACP can hydrolyze trisodium L-ascorbate-2-phosphate salt (AAP) to produce ascorbic acid (AA), which increases colorimetric signal generated by FeOOH@CDs. Interestingly, TA can inhibit ACP activity through strong complexation and reduce the generated AA, by which most ox-TMB could not be restored. Meanwhile, the generated AA could reduce FeOOH to collapse into Fe2+, enabling the assembled CDs dropping-off into solution and restored fluorescence. However, TA can inhibit the ACP activity, which prevents fluorescence recovery. A colorimetric/fluorescence dual-mode sensing was established by cascading ACP/POD mimics. The limits of detection are 0.83 μM and 22.8 μM (3σ/S) for the fluorescence mode and colorimetric mode, respectively. The as-proposed method was applied to detect TA in wine samples, consistent well with the results of HPLC. This work not only expands nanozyme’s potential applications in catalysis and sensing, but also provides a potential platform for rapid TA detection for food control.
coredata.dcterms:subject: FeOOH@CDs nanocomposite
ACP/POD-like cascade
Catalytic mechanism
Colorimetric/fluorescence dual-mode sensor
Tartaric acid

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Study of POD-like and catalytic mechanism of FeOOH NRs, CDs and FeOOH@CDs
Study of POD-like activity and its catalytic mechanism
Detection of TA by colorimetric and fluorescence dual mode

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM images of CDs. (B) TEM images of FeOOH NRs. (C) Elemental mapping of FeOOH NRs. (D) Survey spectrum of FeOOH NRs, (E, F) High-resolution XPS spectra of Fe 2p (E) and O 1 s of FeOOH NRs (F). (G) XRD pattern of FeOOH NRs.
(A) HRTEM image of FeOOH@CDs. (B) Zeta potential of CDs, FeOOH NRs, and FeOOH@CDs. (C) UV–vis spectra of CDs, FeOOH NRs and FeOOH@CDs.

(A) Visible absorption spectra of FeOOH NRs + TMB + H2O2 (a), FeOOH@CDs + TMB + H2O2 (b), FeOOH + TMB (c), FeOOH@CDs + TMB (d), CDs + TMB + H2O2 (e), CDs + TMB (f). (B) Time-dependent absorbance curves of FeOOH@CDs + TMB + H2O2 with and without IPA as the scavenger. (C) Change of FeOOH@CDs relative enzyme activity after adding different scavengers, (D) Fluorescence experiment to test the presence of •OH by fusing terephthalic acid (λex/λem = 315 nm/435 nm). (E) Lineweaver-Burk curve by varying H2O2 concentration while TMB concentration was fixed at 1 mM, (F) Lineweaver-Burk curve by varying TMB concentration while H2O2 concentration was fixed at 0.5 mM. The error bars represent the standard deviation of three independent measurements.
CV responses of FeOOH@CDs and FeOOH@CDs /H2O2. Inset: CV responses of bare GCE and H2O2. (B) Visible spectra of Cyt C only and Cyt C reacted with FeOOH@CDs.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of FeOOH NRs (black line) and fluorescence spectra of CDs (red and blue lines). (B) Fluorescence lifetime curves of CDs in the absence and after assembly on the FeOOH NRs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Visible absorption spectra of Fe2++[ Fe(CN)6]3− (a), Fe3[Fe(CN)6]2 − (b), AA + FeOOH@CDs + [Fe(CN)6]3− (c), ACP + AAP + FeOOH@CDs + [Fe(CN)6]3− (d), Fe2+− (e), [Fe(CN)6]3− (f), AA + FeOOH@CDs− (g), ACP + AAP− (h), AA + FeOOH@CDs (i), ACP + AAP + FeOOH@CDs− (j). (B) Visible spectra of Fe2++Phen (a), ACP + AAP + FeOOH@CDs + Phen (b), AA + FeOOH@CDs + Phen (c), Fe3++Phen (d), Fe2+(e), Phen (f), ACP + AAP + FeOOH@CDs (g). Species concentration: 30 mM Fe2+in (A), 50 μ M Fe2+ in (B), 20 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−, 20 mM AA, 1 mM Phen, 30 mM AAP, 0.6 U mL−1 ACP, 2 mg mL−1 FeOOH@CDs.

(A) Visible absorbance spectra of FeOOH@CDs + TMB + H2O2 (a), TA + AAP + ACP + FeOOH@CDs + TMB + H2O2 (b), AAP + ACP + FeOOH@CDs + TMB + H2O2 (c), AAP + ACP + TMB + H2O2 + TA (d), AAP + ACP + TA (e). (B) Fluorescence spectra of CDs (a), FeOOH@CDs + AAP + ACP (b), TA + FeOOH@CDs + AAP + ACP (c), FeOOH@CDs (d), AAP + ACP + TMB + H2O2 + TA (e), AAP + ACP + TA (f).

(A) Visible absorption spectra with different TA concentrations, (B) Fluorescence spectra of the fluorescence assay based with different TA concentrations, (C) Calibration curve for TA based on colorimetric mode, (D) Calibration curve for TA based on fluorescent mode, (E) Selectivity test for colorimetric mode. (F) Selectivity test for fluorescent mode. In both case, cations and anions in 1 mM, organic species in 5 mM.
Schematic colorimetric/fluorescent TA detection based on FeOOH@CDs nanocomposite.

head.ce:title: Colorimetric/fluorescence dual-mode sensor based on acid phosphatase/peroxidase-mimicking cascade catalysis for ultrasensitive detection of tartaric acid

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Chemical Biology & Biosensing, and College of Life Sciences, Qingdao University, 308 Ningxia Road, Qingdao 266071, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute for Chemical Biology & Biosensing, and College of Life Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute for Chemical Biology & Biosensing, and College of Life Sciences, Qingdao University, 308 Ningxia Road, Qingdao 266071, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanozymes hold great promise in catalysis-based sensing, however, challenges remain due to their less specificity and unclear catalytic mechanisms, which limited their potential applications. Herein we report an ultrasensitive colorimetric/fluorescence dual-mode tartaric acid (TA) sensor based on hydroxy-iron oxide (FeOOH) nanorods (NRs)@carbon dots (CDs) nanocomposite incorporating with acid phosphatase (ACP). The peroxidase (POD)-like activity of FeOOH can catalyze H2O2 to generate OH, which can further oxidize 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to produce blue oxTMB, while the fluorescence of CDs can be quenched by the assembled FeOOH NRs through internal filter effect. The FeOOH@CDs can effectively transfer electron enabling high catalytic activity, which is supported by Cytochrome c as an electron transporter. ACP can hydrolyze trisodium L-ascorbate-2-phosphate salt (AAP) to produce ascorbic acid (AA), which increases colorimetric signal generated by FeOOH@CDs. Interestingly, TA can inhibit ACP activity through strong complexation and reduce the generated AA, by which most ox-TMB could not be restored. Meanwhile, the generated AA could reduce FeOOH to collapse into Fe2+, enabling the assembled CDs dropping-off into solution and restored fluorescence. However, TA can inhibit the ACP activity, which prevents fluorescence recovery. A colorimetric/fluorescence dual-mode sensing was established by cascading ACP/POD mimics. The limits of detection are 0.83 μM and 22.8 μM (3σ/S) for the fluorescence mode and colorimetric mode, respectively. The as-proposed method was applied to detect TA in wine samples, consistent well with the results of HPLC. This work not only expands nanozyme’s potential applications in catalysis and sensing, but also provides a potential platform for rapid TA detection for food control.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanozymes are an emerging class of inorganic catalysts that can mimic the catalytic activity of natural enzymes [1]. They possess high catalytic efficiency, cost-effectiveness, stability, adaptability, making them highly promising in many fields such as biomedicine, environmental remediation and sensing [2,3]. To date, various transition metal compound-based nanozymes have been developed, including Fe3O4 [4], Co3O4 [5,6], MoS2 [7,8], MnO2 [9,10] and CeO2 [11,12]. Transition hydroxide metal compounds (MOOH, M = Co, Fe, Al, Ni) are a special type of metal compound based nanomaterials, which have excellent light absorption ability, good environmental compatibility and a large specific surface area [13]. These excellent properties make MOOH a potential quencher for fluorescent groups. In their crystal structure, the M3+ are surrounded by oxides (O2−), and form hydrogen bonds or coordination bonds with hydroxyl (OH). Strong reducing substances can reduce M3+ to M2+ which makes the material itself disintegrates. Among them, FeOOH has unique crystal phase structure and surface properties, which has been widely applied in catalysis, sensing, energy conversion and environmental purification [13–17]. Although nanozyme-based sensing platforms have been widely reported, it is still challenging to address the poor selectivity[18,19]. The key issue lies in the facts that, different from natural enzymes, nanozymes lack of specific molecular binding sites. So sensing based on cascading nanozymes with natural enzymes by utilizing the specific binding of natural enzymes to inhibitors is an effective measure to solve the specificity problem [20–22].

Tartaric acid (2,3-dihydroxybutanedioic acid, TA) is a major organic acid found in wine and is widely used in industries such as food, pharmaceuticals and cosmetics [23]. The level of TA directly influences the sensory characteristics, stability, and microbiological properties of wine [24,25]. With the improvement of people's living standards, the demand for wine is increasing, and the abnormal content of TA in wine may lead to allergic reactions and renal dysfunction [26]. Therefore, it is necessary to monitor TA in wine. Currently, various methods have been reported for detecting TA, including high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [27], electrochemical methods [28,29], capillary electrophoresis [30] and chemiluminescence [31,32]. However, these methods are either complicated, time-consuming, or cost-expensive. Particularly, these methods basically involve the output of one signal mode, which is difficult to guarantee the accuracy of detection. The dual-mode sensor can verify each other, which would greatly improve the accuracy and reliability of detection and makes it suitable for the detection of complex samples[33–35].

In this contribution, we report that FeOOH nanorods (NRs) exhibit excellent POD-like activity, on which fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) can be assembled by electrostatic attraction to form FeOOH@CDs nanocomposite. FeOOH@CDs restores the POD mimicking activity, while the fluorescence of CDs is quenched. On the basis of acid phosphatase (ACP) hydrolyzing trisodium L-ascorbate-2-phosphate salt (AAP) to produce the reducing substance ascorbic acid (AA), which decreases the colorimetric signal generated by the blue oxTMB by oxidizing 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) by POD-like of FeOOH@CDs, while TA can inhibit ACP activity through strong complexation and reduce the amount of AA generated, by which most ox-TMB could not be restored to TMB, and the colorimetric signal keeps stable. Nevertheless, the AA generated by the ACP-catalyzed substrate could reduce the FeOOH NRs to collapse into Fe2+, which makes the assembled CDs detach from FeOOH NRs to restore the fluorescence. The presence of TA can inhibit ACP activity, which prevent the fluorescence from the CDs recovery (Scheme 1 ). Thus a colorimetric/fluorescence dual model sensing platform for TA was established. The as-developed method was applicable to detect TA in wine samples satisfactorily.

Iron chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O), N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF), anhydrous citric acid, hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) (30 % wt), ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid disodium salt (Na2EDTA), potassium ferricyanide and thiourea were bought from Sinopharm Chemical Co. (Shanghai, China). ACP was purchased from Tokyo Kasei Kogyo Co. (Tokyo, Japan). TA, o-phenanthroline (Phen) and Cytochrome c (Cyt C) were purchased from Yuanye Biotechnology Co. (Shanghai, China). Trisodium L-ascorbate-2-phosphate salt (AAP), ascorbic acid (AA) and polyethyleneimine (PEI, MW:70000) were purchased from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., LTD. (Shanghai, China). TMB was purchased from Ti Xi’ai Co. (Shanghai, China). Methyl orange (MO), p-benzoquinone (BQ), sodium azide (NaN3) and isopropanol (IPA) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, USA). The sample wine was purchased from Yantai Changyu Winemaking Co., LTD (Yantai, China).

Scanning electron microscope (SEM) images were obtained via a JSM-2100 Plus scanning electron microscope (JEC, Japan). The transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were taken with a Tecnai F-20 S-TWIN transmission electron microscope (FEI, USA). The powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were detected by a D8 diffractometer (Bruker, Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed by using Versa Probe III (ULVAC-PHI, Japan). The SPARK 10 M (Tecan, Switzerland) microplate reader was applied to measure UV–vis spectra and absorbance. The Zetasizer Nano ZSE (Malvern, UK) was used to measure the surface potential of the particles. High Performance Liquid Chromatography (HPLC) was measured by UltiMate 3000 (Thermo Fisher, UK). The fluorescence spectra were measured by F-7000 fluorescence photometer (Hitachi, Japan).

500 μL of PEI was dispersed in 100 mL of deionized water by stirring. Then 7.1 g of FeCl3·6H2O was added with magnetic stirring for 30 min to generate homogeneous solution. The solution was then transferred to a three-necked flask and reacted at 80 ℃ for 2 h in an oil bath, during which the orange-red solution was transformed to a yellow suspension. The resultant solution was cooled down to room temperature and centrifugated to collect the precipitate, which was followed by washing with ethanol and water three times. Finally the product was freeze-dried.

CDs were synthesized by a hydrothermal method reported [36]. Briefly, 4.0 g of thiourea and 2.0 g of anhydrous citric acid were dissolved in 20 mL of DMF with strong stirring for 30 min. And then the mixed solution was transferred to a 50 mL Teflon autoclave, which was heated at 160 ℃ for 6 h before cooling down to room temperature. Finally, the product was centrifuged and washed to collect the precipitate and freeze-dried.

40 mg of FeOOH NRs and 3.2 mg of CDs were dissolved in 4 mL water and continuously sonicated for 6 h. This mixed suspension was then centrifuged to get the precipitate, which was washed with pure water three times to remove any unbound CDs. The precipitate was finally collected and freeze-dried.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of POD-like and catalytic mechanism of FeOOH NRs, CDs and FeOOH@CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To study the POD-like activity, FeOOH NRs (100 μg/mL), or CDs (100 μg/mL), or FeOOH@CDs (100 μg/mL) were separately mixed with H2O2 (300 μM) and TMB (400 μM) with acetate buffer (0.2 M, pH 4) and incubated at 25 ℃ for 15 min. The spectra were recorded with control experiments.

The steady-state kinetics of the POD-like of the FeOOH@CDs is based on the Michaelis-Menten equation:
(1)
v =
V
m a x
[ S ]
K m
+
[ S ]
In Eq.1, [S], V max, K m, and ν are substrate concentration, maximum reaction rate, Michaelis constant, and initial rate, separately.

System I: ACP (4 U/mL, 15 μL), 10 μL of TA with different concentrations and pH 4.8 acetate buffer (200 mM, 65 μL) were incubated for 10 min in a water bath at 37°C. Then AAP (20 mM, 10 μL) was added and incubated for another 20 min. Controls were performed without TA.

System II: FeOOH@CDs (200 μg/mL, 30 μL), TMB (5 mM, 15 μL), H2O2 (6 mM, 15 μL), pH 4.8 acetate buffer (200 mM, 10 μL) were incubated at 25 ℃ for 15 min, during which the absorbance at 652 nm (A652 nm) was detected until a plateau reached (the A652 nm remained stable) and the absorbance was denoted as A1. Then 30 μL of the solution from System I was added to System II and incubated for 5 min. After that the A652nm was measured. The A652 nm for the control group without TA was denoted as A0, while the absorbance with different concentrations of TA was denoted as A. The difference between A and A0 was recorded as ΔA. The working curve was plotted by ΔA/A0 as a function of TA concentration.

TA with varying concentrations (15 μL), ACP (5 U/mL, 30 μL) and pH 4.8 acetate buffer (200 mM, 30 μL) were incubated in 37 ℃ water bath for 10 min, to which AAP (10 mM, 30 μL) was added and incubated for 20 min. Control experiments were performed without TA. Then FeOOH@CDs (1.4 mg/mL, 30 μL) was added and incubated for another 20 min. At excitation wavelength (λex) of 360 nm, the fluorescence spectra were recorded at emission wavelength (λem) of 440 nm. The fluorescence intensity at 440 nm in the presence of TA is denoted as F, while the fluorescence intensity for the control group is denoted as F0. F-F0 was denoted as ΔF. The working curve is drawn with ΔF/F0 (%) as a function of TA concentration.

Under the optimized conditions, standard addition method was performed. The local wines were first filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane to remove any precipitates, which were then diluted 20 times before measurement. Standard TA solution was added to the sample solution, and then calculated recovery after measurement. Each sample was detected independently three times. HPLC results were compared.

CDs were synthesized by a simple hydrothermal method by using thiourea as a carbon source, citric acid as a surface modifier, and DMF is used as a solvent and auxiliary agent to provide a favorable environment. From TEM image, CDs are uniformly distributed with a particle size of about 8 nm (Fig. 1 A). FeOOH NRs are synthesized by chemical precipitation via the coordination reaction of the amine group in PEI with Fe3+, which was surrounded by oxides (O2−), and then form hydrogen bonds or coordination bonds with hydroxyl to aggregate FeOOH NRs. FeOOH NRs present a regular rod-like structure with a length of about 200 nm and good crystallization (Fig. 1B). The lattice spacing of 0.22 nm corresponds to the (301) crystal plane of FeOOH NRs [37]. EDS analysis showed that Fe and O elements were evenly distributed in FeOOH NRs (Fig. 1C). The element composition and valence states of FeOOH NRs were further analyzed by XPS spectra. The electron spectral peaks of Fe and O appeared in the XPS survey spectrum (Fig. 1D), once again proving that FeOOH NRs are composed of Fe and O elements. In the Fe2p XPS spectrum (Fig. 1E), the peaks of 711.4 eV and 724.6 eV are attributed to Fe (Ⅲ) 2p3/2 and 2p1/2, respectively. In the O1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 1F), peaks of 530 eV and 530.3 eV belong to Fe-O-F and Fe-O-H [14], respectively. The XRD diffraction pattern (Fig. 1G) shows strong sharp diffraction peaks at 2θ = 12° (110), 26.9° (310), 35.4° (211), 39.4° (301), 56.2° (521), which are consistent with the FeOOH standard card JDPD (No 34.1266), indicating that the product is well-crystallized FeOOH NRs.

Observed from the TEM image of FeOOH@CDs nanocomposite, CDs were uniformly distributed on the FeOOH NRs (Fig. 2 A). HRTEM revealed the lattice structure of the CDs with a lattice spacing of 0.26 nm corresponding to the (100) plane of graphite. Considering that CDs were synthesized using citric acid with carboxyl groups, CDs have an overall negative charge around –22.8 eV (Fig. 2 B). While FeOOH was synthesized using PEI as the precursor with abundance of amine groups, which imparts a positive charge around + 30 eV to the FeOOH NRs (Fig. 2 B). The charge value of FeOOH@CDs is around −15 eV, indicating a significant change in the Zeta potential compared to those of FeOOH NRs and CDs alone, which demonstrates many CD particles assembling onto FeOOH NRs by electrostatic attraction. In the UV–vis spectra analysis of FeOOH NRs and CDs, a broad absorption peak appears at 390 nm for FeOOH NRs, while CDs exhibit an absorption peak at 350 nm. However, after formation of the nanocomposite, both the typical peaks for FeOOH NRs and CDs significantly decreased (Fig. 2 C). Further, FT-IR spectra of FeOOH, CDs and FeOOH@CDs were compared (Fig.S1). CDs contain the carbonyl group (C=O) with vibration peak near 2000 cm−1. The basic structure of FeOOH contains Fe-O bond with typical lattice vibration peak at around 600 cm−1. The fact that FeOOH@CDs have the characteristic lattice vibration peak of FeOOH and CDs. Thus the characterization results indicate the successful preparation of the FeOOH@CDs nanocomposite.

The CDs’ fluorescence spectrum was investigated. With the increase of λex, the fluorescence intensity of CDs at λem = 440 nm increases and becomes highest at λex = 360 nm (Fig. S2A). So the optimal λex and λem of CDs are 360 nm and 440 nm, respectively (Fig. S2B). The fluorescence stability of CDs was studied by continuous fluorescence time scanning. After 20 min, there was no obvious attenuation of CDs fluorescence (Fig. S2C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of POD-like activity and its catalytic mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: POD-like can catalyze H2O2 to oxidize TMB to form ox-TMB, which presents a characteristic absorption peak at 652 nm. As shown in Fig. 3 A, FeOOH/TMB/H2O2 (curve a) and FeOOH@CDs/TMB/H2O2 (curve b) present strong absorption peak at 652 nm, while the systems of FeOOH/TMB, CDs/TMB/H2O2 and the other control groups show ignorable signals (curves c-f), indicating that FeOOH NRs exhibits POD-like activity and does not show oxidase-like activity. The composite FeOOH@CDs can still exhibit excellent POD-like activity, indicating the loading of CDs has no effect on the POD-like activity of FeOOH NRs.

To make clear which reactive oxygen species (ROS) responsible for FeOOH@CDs exhibiting POD-like activity, a series of scavenging experiments were done. Generally, IPA is used as a scavenger of •OH [6], BQ as a scavenger of O2 •− [38], NaN3 as a scavenger of 1O2 [8], and EDTA as a scavenger of oxygen vacancy [5]. The POD-like activity of FeOOH@CDs decreases significantly upon addition of scavenger IPA (Fig. 3B), while other quenchers such as NaN3 (Fig. S3A), EDTA (Fig. S3B) and BQ (Fig. S3C) have little effect on POD-like activity, suggesting that the POD mimics of FeOOH@CDs may be due to the production of •OH. To further verify the POD-like mechanism of FeOOH@CD, the experiment of terephthalic acid capturing •OH fluorescence was performed. It has been reported that terephthalic acid can react with •OH to form 2-hydroxyterephthalic acid, which has a strong fluorescence emission peak at 435 nm and can be used as a sensitive probe of •OH [39]. The fluorescence intensity of FeOOH@CDs at 435 nm in the presence of FeOOH@CDs was greatly higher than that of the control group (Fig. 3D), which further confirms that •OH is responsible for FeOOH@CDs producing POD-like.

Under the optimal conditions (pH 4, 400 μM TMB, 300 μM H2O2 and incubation 10 min, Fig.S4), the enzymatic reaction kinetics of FeOOH@CDs was studied by drawing the Michaelis-Menten curve (Fig. 3 E-F). The Km and Vmax for TMB are calculated to be 0.21 mM and 0.182 μM/s, respectively, while the K m and V max for H2O2 are 0.21 mM and 0.32 μM/s, respectively. Table S1 summarizes the kinetic constants of HRP and other POD-like nanozymes. It is found that FeOOH@CDs has roughly equivalent substrate affinity to Fe-single atomic nanozyme [40], whether TMB or H2O2 is used as the substrate. In addition, the Vmax for FeOOH@CDs is 3.7 times higher than HRP [4]. The Vmax /Km value is an index to evaluate the catalytic efficiency [39], and the V max/K m value (H2O2 as substrate) of FeOOH@CDs is 1.78 × 10−3 s−1, about 74 times higher than that of HRP. Thus, FeOOH@CDs is an excellent POD mimics.

The electron transfer ability of nanozyme may be one of the important factors for its high enzyme activity [41]. The electrochemical reduction behavior of H2O2 was analyzed by cyclic voltammetry (CV) [17]. Compared with bare glass carbon electrode (GCE), the FeOOH@CDs modified GCE can effectively catalyze H2O2 (Fig. 4 A). The electron acceptability of FeOOH@CDs was investigated using Cyt C as an electron transporter. The reduced Cyt C has characteristic peaks at 520 nm and 550 nm. When Cyt C was incubated with FeOOH@CDs, the two peaks disappeared, and a new peak appeared at 530 nm (Fig. 4B), representing the oxidation state Cyt C [42]. The above results show that FeOOH@CDs can effectively transfer electron, enabling high catalytic activity.

CDs can aggregate onto FeOOH NRs by electrostatic interaction, which was confirmed by TEM image (Fig. 2). The assembled FeOOH NRs can induce CDs’ fluorescence quenching, due to the absorption spectrum of FeOOH NRs and the excitation spectrum of CDs significantly overlap (Fig. 5 A). The fluorescence lifetime (τ) of CDs is 7.92 ns, while that of FeOOH@CDs is 7.66 ns (Fig. 5B). That is, the τ remains almost constant after CDs assembling on the FeOOH NRs, indicating that the mechanism of fluorescence quenching is internal filter effect [43–45].

It is reported that hydroxyl metal oxides can be decomposed into ions by strong reducing substances [46,47]. So we proposed that AA can reduce FeOOH to Fe2+ while AA is oxidized to dehydroascorbic acid (Eq.2) [48]:
(2)

Observed from TEM image, after FeOOH@CD incubation with AA, the CDs nanoparticle on the FeOOH NRs were disappeared and the regular rod turned into irregular shape (Fig.S5), indicating the etching the NR surface. To verify that Fe2+ is produced, two complexing agents of potassium ferriccyanide and Phen were selected to test, considering that they can react with Fe2+ to generate colorful complexes with typical absorption peaks. When Fe2+ is added in [Fe(CN6]3− solution, a blue precipitate Fe3[Fe(CN)6]2 is produced (Eq.3), which has a characteristic absorption band at 740 nm. Then when potassium ferricyanide is added to either AA/FeOOH@CD system or the ACP/AAP/FeOOH@CD system, a blue precipitate is also observed (Fig. 6 A), suggesting that the reduced Fe2+ complexes with ferricyanide ion to generate Fe3[Fe (CN)6]2. Fe2+ reacts to Phen to generate red Fe(Phen)3 2+ complex (Eq.4). Similarly, when Phen is added into AA/FeOOH@CD or ACP/AAP/FeOOH@CDs system, the solution becomes red with a characteristic absorption peak at 510 nm (Fig. 6B), indicating that the reduced Fe2+ reacts to Phen to generate red Fe(Phen)3 2+ complex. Thus the above characterizations verified that AA produced by AAP to etch the surface of FeOOH NRs to generate Fe2+.
(3)
3
F e
2 +
+ 2
[
F e
C N
6
]
3 -
→
F e
3
[ F e
C N
6
]
2
(4)

Metal ions often lead to fluorescence quenching. In order to verify the effect of Fe2+ on the fluorescence of CDs, a series of concentrations of Fe2+ were mixed with CDs to test the fluorescence of CDs. It was found that Fe2+ had no obvious effect on the fluorescence of CDs (Fig.S6).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of TA by colorimetric and fluorescence dual mode
ce:section.ce:para: FeOOH@CDs + TMB + H2O2 exhibit strong ox-TMB signal (Fig. 7 A, curve a). The ox-TMB signal of AAP/ACP/FeOOH@CDs/TMB/H2O2 system is extremely weak (Fig. 7A, curve c), indicating that ACP catalyzed AAP to produce AA, which can reduce ox-TMB to form TMB, and made the colorimetric signal decrease. The underlying reason is that the reducing power generated by AA to capture ∙OH [49]. However, the colorimetric signal of ox-TMB in AAP/ACP/FeOOH@CDs/TMB/H2O2/TA system did not significantly decrease (Fig. 7A, curve b), indicating that TA inhibited the activity of ACP, for which the amount of AA produced by AAP catalyzed by ACP was decreased. Accordingly, most ox-TMB could not be restored to TMB, so the colorimetric signal decreased. It is reasonable, considering that TA contains many functional groups such as carboxyl (–COOH) and hydroxyl (–OH), which has extremely powerful chelating ability [50–52], which can easily chelate Zn2+/Mg2+ in the active center of natural metalloenzyme ACP through complexation [53], reducing its enzyme activity [54–56]. CDs shows a strong fluorescence emission peak at 440 nm (Fig. 7B, curve a), while FeOOH@CDs exhibits very weak fluorescence (Fig. 7B, curve d). However, FeOOH@CDs/AAP/ACP system shows a significant fluorescence recovery (Fig. 7B, curve b), indicating that ACP catalyzed AA produced by AAP to promote the fluorescence recovery of CDs in FeOOH@CDs. TA inhibited the activity of ACP and reduced the production of AA, so that the fluorescence of FeOOH@CDs/AAP/ACP system did not show obvious recovery (Fig. 7B, curve c). The above phenomenon may be applicable to explore the dual-mode sensing platform.

The conditions of the dual-mode sensing system were optimized (Fig.S7). On the basis of the optimal conditions, the working curves can be explored. A652nm increases with the increase of TA concentration in the system, and the solution gradually takes on a dark blue color (Fig. 8 A). As can be seen from the working curve (Fig. 8C), the ΔA/A0 percentage is linear with TA concentration with two parts of 0.05–2 mM and 2–70 mM, and the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated as 22.8 μM (3σ/S). For the fluorescence mode, the peak fluorescence intensity at 440 nm decreased with the increase of TA concentration in the system, and the solution gradually became colorless under ultraviolet irradiation (Fig. 8B). The ΔF/F0 percentage is linear with TA concentration with two parts of 0.001–0.03 mM and 0.05–90 mM (Fig. 8D), and LOD is 0.83 μM (3σ/S). Compared with other detection methods reported so far (Table S2), the colorimetric method and fluorescence method proposed in this paper have lower LODs, among which the fluorescence mode has a LOD 7-fold lower than the HPLC method reported [57]. The as-proposed dual-mode sensor platform has a wide linear range, covering the concentration range of TA (13.3–66.7 mM) in most wines (red, white, ice wines, etc.) on the market [58].

Selectivity and anti-interference ability are necessary indexes to evaluate the performance of detection methods. Therefore, other hydroxy-carboxylic acids such as malic acid, citric acid, glutaric acid and gallic acid were selected for selective experiments, and it was found that the dual-mode sensor did not respond significantly to these TA analogues (Fig. 8E,F). In addition, the possibility of interfering substances in the actual sample wine was analyzed, including anions, ethanol, glucose, AA and tannic acid. The concentration of interfering substances was much higher than the actual concentration of interfering substances in wine (5 ∼ 10 times) [59]. Although some reducing substances such as AA and tannic acid exist in wine, their concentrations are much lower than that of TA. After dilution pretreatment, they did not significantly enhance the colorimetric signal, and reduced the fluorescence signal of the detection system (Fig. 8E, F). Therefore, the dual-mode TA sensing has good selectivity and strong anti-interference ability.

The TA concentration in the sample wine was obtained by HPLC (Supplementary data). According to the different distribution coefficients of the components in the wine in the stationary phase (C18 reverse column) and the mobile phase (methanol and diammonium hydrogen phosphate), there was a significant difference in retention time, and TA was distinguished at 2.888 min (Fig. S8A). The calibration curve of TA was drawn with the concentration of TA as the horizontal coordinate and the peak area as the vertical coordinate (Fig. S8B). In order to verify the practicability of the proposed dual-mode sensor platform, the TA concentration in local wines were determined by standard addition method. Table S3 shows the results, which are in agreement with HPLC. Further, the methods exhibit recoveries between 94–108 % and RSD less than 7 %, indicating that the method has repeatability and reliability in the actual sample detection.

In conclusion, FeOOH@CDs nanocomposite with excellent POD-like activity was prepared. By cascading the natural enzyme alkaline phosphatase with the FeOOH@CDs based POD mimics, a rapid, selective and sensitive colorimetric/fluorescent dual-mode sensing system was developed for the detection of tartaric acid. Further, the catalytic mechanism and colorimetric sensing mechanism were intensively investigated. The colorimetric mode shows two linear parts of 0.05–2 mM and 2–70 mM TA with the LOD of 22.8 μM (3σ/S), while the fluorescence mode shows two linear ranges of 0.001–0.03 mM and 0.03–90 mM TA with the LOD of 0.83 μM (3σ/S). The as-proposed method can be applied to detect TA in wine samples selectively, consistent well with the results by HPLC. This system holds the potential for further promotion in food and pharmaceuticals, contributing to the advancement of food control.

Xin Zhang: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation. Xin Liu: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation. Ziting Lin: Investigation. Guangze Sun: Investigation. Zihan Bian: Investigation. Huan Liu: Investigation. Aihua Liu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2025.113850.

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Stec, K.M. Holtz, E.R. Kantrowitz, A revised mechanism for the alkaline phosphatase reaction involving three metal ions, Journal of Molecular Biology, 299(2000) 1303-1311.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Method development for the determination of seven organic acids in wines by reversed-phase high performance liquid chromatography
Chromatographia

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Zotou, Z. Loukou, O. Karava, Method development for the determination of seven organic acids in wines by reversed-phase high performance liquid chromatography, Chromatographia, 60(2004).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Investigating the role of tartaric acid in wine astringency
Food Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. Zhao, G. Du, S. Wang, P. Zhao, X. Cao, C. Cheng, et al., Investigating the role of tartaric acid in wine astringency, Food Chemistry, 403(2023) 134385.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ascorbic acid: A review of its chemistry and reactivity in relation to a wine environment
Crit. Rev. Food Sci. Nutr.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.P. Bradshaw, C. Barril, A.C. Clark, P.D. Prenzler, G.R. Scollary, Ascorbic acid: A review of its chemistry and reactivity in relation to a wine environment, Critical Reviews in Food Science and Nutrition, 51(2011) 479-498.
